title: Evidence
subject: Evidence
summary: Evidence covers relevance, character evidence, impeachment, opinion testimony, authentication, privileges, hearsay, and constitutional limits on proof at trial.

sections.title: Current Rules Note
body: This outline reflects the Federal Rules of Evidence currently in force as of July 2026, including the amendments effective December 1, 2023 and December 1, 2024. It does not apply proposed or future amendments before their effective dates.
Evidence law determines what information the judge or jury may consider at trial and how that information must be presented.
The main MBE question is usually:
Why is this evidence being offered, and does a rule admit or exclude it for that purpose?
Authentication and the best evidence rule

sections.title: The Evidence MBE Attack Plan
body: For every evidence question, ask these questions in order:
Is there a special exclusionary rule?
If hearsay, is it nonhearsay or within an exception?
Does the best evidence rule apply?
Is a privilege or constitutional rule involved?

sections.title: When the Federal Rules of Evidence Apply
body: When the Federal Rules of Evidence Apply
The Federal Rules of Evidence generally apply in federal:
Courts of appeals when evidence rules are relevant
Except for privilege rules, the Federal Rules generally do not apply when the court is deciding:
At a sentencing hearing, the judge considers information that would ordinarily be inadmissible hearsay at trial.
The ordinary hearsay rules generally do not control that sentencing decision.
The Rules govern trials, but not every judicial proceeding.

sections.title: The Judge’s Role in Preliminary Questions
body: The Judge’s Role in Preliminary Questions
The judge decides preliminary questions involving:
Whether an expert’s methods are reliable
The judge generally decides these questions by a preponderance of the evidence.
When deciding these preliminary questions, the judge is not bound by the Evidence Rules except for privilege rules.
The judge may consider evidence that would not itself be admissible before the jury.
The prosecution offers a statement as one made during a conspiracy.
The judge may consider the statement and other proof when deciding whether the conspiracy existed, although the statement alone cannot establish the conspiracy.
The judge screens admissibility and may look behind the evidentiary curtain.

sections.title: The Jury’s Role
body: After evidence is admitted, the jury generally decides:
Whether a witness actually perceived the event
The judge decides that enough evidence exists for a jury to find that a signature is authentic.
The jury may still decide that the signature is forged.
The judge decides whether evidence may enter; the jury decides what to believe.

sections.title: Conditional Relevance
body: Sometimes evidence is relevant only if another fact exists.
The proponent must introduce evidence sufficient to support a finding that the connecting fact exists.
The judge does not decide that the connecting fact is actually true.
The judge asks whether a reasonable jury could find it true.
A prosecutor offers a key allegedly found in the defendant’s backpack to connect the defendant to a storage unit.
The key matters only if it opens that unit.
The judge may admit it conditionally, subject to later proof that it fits the lock.
Conditional relevance requires enough evidence for the jury to find the missing link.

sections.title: Hearings Outside the Jury’s Presence
body: A preliminary hearing must be conducted outside the jury’s presence when:
It concerns the admissibility of a confession
A criminal defendant who testifies on a preliminary issue requests it
Justice requires exclusion of the jury
A criminal defendant who testifies only about a preliminary admissibility issue does not automatically become subject to cross-examination on the entire case.
A defendant may contest admissibility without opening the whole defense.

sections.title: Preserving an Objection to Admitted Evidence
body: Preserving an Objection to Admitted Evidence
When evidence is admitted, a party generally must:
Make a timely objection or motion to strike
State the specific ground unless it is obvious from context
Show that the ruling affected a substantial right
An objection should normally be made after the question is asked but before the witness answers.
If the answer itself creates the problem, counsel should move to strike.
A witness unexpectedly gives hearsay in response to a proper question.
The opponent should move to strike the answer.
Object before the answer; move to strike after the damage appears.

sections.title: Preserving Error When Evidence Is Excluded
body: Preserving Error When Evidence Is Excluded
When evidence is excluded, the proponent generally must make an offer of proof.
What the evidence would have shown
No offer is required when the substance is apparent from context.
The judge bars testimony about a witness’s financial arrangement with a party.
Counsel should state on the record that the testimony would show the witness is paid only if the party wins, making the testimony relevant to bias.
Excluded evidence needs a record explaining what the jury would have heard.

sections.title: Definitive Rulings and Plain Error
body: Once the judge definitively rules on admissibility, the party need not renew the objection or offer of proof at trial.
An appellate court may correct an obvious evidentiary error affecting substantial rights even without a preserved objection.
A final pretrial ruling preserves the issue; plain error rescues only serious unpreserved mistakes.

sections.title: Limited Admissibility
body: For one purpose but not another
Against one party but not another
Upon timely request, the judge must restrict the evidence to its proper scope and give a limiting instruction.
A witness’s unsworn prior inconsistent statement is admitted to impeach credibility but not to prove the statement’s truth.
The judge should instruct the jury to use it only for impeachment.
One item of evidence may wear different legal labels for different purposes.

sections.title: Rule of Completeness
body: When one party introduces part of a statement, the opponent may require introduction of another part that fairness requires the jury to consider at the same time.
The completing material may be admitted over a hearsay objection when necessary to correct a misleading impression.
Related statements that clarify the introduced portion
A prosecutor offers a recording in which the defendant says, “I took the money.”
The next sentence is, “I took it from the desk only because the owner told me to deposit it.”
The defense may require the clarifying sentence to be heard immediately.
A party cannot create a false picture by presenting only the damaging half.

sections.title: Judicial Notice
body: Judicial notice allows the court to accept an adjudicative fact without formal proof.
The fact must not be reasonably disputable because it is:
Generally known within the court’s territorial jurisdiction, or
Accurately and readily determined from a source whose accuracy cannot reasonably be questioned
The judge may not rely solely on private personal knowledge.
A court may judicially notice the day of the week on which a specific historical date fell.
Judicial notice is for indisputable facts, not shortcuts around disputed proof.

sections.title: Judicial Notice Procedure
body: The court may take judicial notice:
If a party supplies the necessary information and the fact qualifies, the court must take notice.
A party is entitled to an opportunity to be heard.
The jury must accept the noticed fact as conclusive.
The jury may, but is not required to, accept the noticed fact as conclusive.
Conclusive in civil cases; optional for the criminal jury.

sections.title: Illustrative Aids
body: An illustrative aid helps the factfinder understand evidence or argument but is not itself offered as substantive evidence.
The court may permit the aid if its usefulness is not substantially outweighed by risks such as:
The aid should be placed in the record when practicable.
It generally may not go into the jury room unless all parties consent or the court finds good cause.
An illustrative aid explains evidence; it is not evidence.

sections.title: Order and Control of Trial
body: The judge controls the mode and order of presenting evidence to:
Make procedures effective for determining truth
Protect witnesses from harassment or undue embarrassment
If the judge calls a witness, every party may cross-examine that witness.
Parties present the case, but the judge manages the courtroom.

sections.title: Scope of Cross-Examination
body: The subject matter of direct examination
The court may permit inquiry into additional matters.
When cross-examination goes beyond direct, the questioning is generally conducted as though on direct examination.
Cross normally follows direct plus credibility.

sections.title: Redirect and Recross
body: Redirect generally addresses significant new matters raised on cross-examination.
Recross generally addresses significant new matters raised on redirect.
The court has discretion to permit broader questioning.
Each later examination responds to the new ground opened immediately before it.

sections.title: Leading Questions
body: A leading question suggests the desired answer.
Leading questions are generally prohibited on direct examination.
To help a child or person with communication difficulty
With a witness identified with an adverse party
Leading questions are generally allowed on cross-examination.
“Your car was traveling more than 60 miles per hour, correct?” is leading.
“How fast was the car traveling?” is not.
Lead on cross, not ordinarily on direct.

sections.title: Improper Questions
body: Common improper questions include those that are:
Based on facts not in evidence
“You ignored the warning, sped away, and never called for help, didn’t you?” is compound because it asks several questions at once.
One supported factual question at a time.

sections.title: Excluding Witnesses from the Courtroom
body: At a party’s request, the court must generally exclude witnesses so they cannot hear other testimony.
The court may also do so on its own.
Persons Who Cannot Be Excluded Under This Rule
A designated representative of an entity party
A person essential to presenting a party’s case
A person whose presence is authorized by statute
The court may also prohibit excluded witnesses from receiving reports or recordings of trial testimony.
Sequestration prevents witnesses from tailoring testimony.

sections.title: Burden of Production
body: The burden of production is the obligation to produce enough evidence for a reasonable factfinder to rule in the party’s favor.
Failure to meet it may result in judgment as a matter of law.
The burden of production may shift during the case.
Production asks whether there is enough evidence to reach the factfinder.

sections.title: Burden of Persuasion
body: The burden of persuasion is the obligation to convince the factfinder.
Some issues require clear and convincing evidence.
The prosecution must prove each element beyond a reasonable doubt.
Production gets the issue to the jury; persuasion wins the issue.

sections.title: Rebuttable Presumptions
body: In an ordinary federal civil case, a rebuttable presumption shifts the burden of production, not the ultimate burden of persuasion.
Once the opponent produces sufficient contrary evidence, the presumption generally disappears under the bursting-bubble approach.
Proof that a properly addressed and stamped letter was mailed creates a presumption that it was received.
Evidence of a reliable mailroom failure may satisfy the opposing burden of production.
A rebuttable presumption pushes evidence forward but usually does not move the final burden.

sections.title: Presumptions in Diversity Cases
body: When state law supplies the rule of decision for a claim or defense, state law governs the effect of a presumption concerning that claim or defense.
Substantive state presumptions follow the state-law claim into federal court.

sections.title: Destruction of Evidence
body: Intentional destruction of relevant evidence may support an inference that the evidence was unfavorable to the destroying party.
Due diligence in seeking or preserving the evidence
Intentional destruction may allow the factfinder to assume the evidence was harmful.

sections.title: Relevance
body: Evidence is relevant when it is both:
Probative — it has any tendency to make a fact more or less probable
Material — the fact matters to the legal dispute
Evidence may be one small brick in a larger wall of proof.
In a dispute over whether a driver could see a cyclist, evidence that the streetlight nearest the collision was broken is relevant.
Any tendency + fact of consequence = relevance.

sections.title: Direct and Circumstantial Evidence
body: Direct evidence proves a fact without requiring an inference.
Circumstantial evidence proves a fact through an inference from another fact.
A witness who saw a person sign a contract gives direct evidence of signing.
A fingerprint on the signature page is circumstantial evidence connecting the person to the document.
Direct evidence says what happened; circumstantial evidence points toward what happened.

sections.title: Rule 403
body: Relevant evidence may be excluded when its probative value is substantially outweighed by a danger of:
A clear autopsy diagram may be admitted to explain wounds.
Dozens of repetitive, graphic photographs may be excluded as cumulative and unfairly inflammatory.
Exclusion requires the danger to substantially outweigh probative value.

sections.title: Availability of Alternative Proof
body: When evaluating probative value under Rule 403, the court may consider whether less prejudicial evidence can prove the same point.
A defendant offers to stipulate to the status element of an offense.
A detailed description of the defendant’s inflammatory prior conviction may add little beyond the stipulation and create substantial unfair prejudice.
Probative value may shrink when a cleaner substitute proves the same fact.

sections.title: Curative Admission
body: When inadmissible evidence was wrongly admitted, the court may permit otherwise inadmissible rebuttal evidence to cure the resulting unfairness.
One evidentiary wrong may justify limited rebuttal, but not unlimited retaliation.

sections.title: Foundation
body: A foundation is the preliminary proof needed before evidence may be admitted.
Before evidence proves the case, the proponent must prove the evidence is fit to use.

blocks.text: A foundation is the preliminary proof needed before evidence may be admitted.

sections.title: Character Evidence: General Rule
body: Character evidence describes a person’s general disposition or trait.
Evidence of character is generally inadmissible to prove that the person acted in conformity with that trait on a particular occasion.
This is the forbidden propensity inference:
They are that kind of person, so they probably acted that way this time.
Character is usually not a shortcut to conduct.

sections.title: Character as an Essential Element
body: Character evidence is admissible when the character trait itself is an essential element of a claim, charge, or defense.
When character is an essential element, it may be proved through:
In a negligent-entrustment case, the driver’s history of dangerous driving may be offered because the driver’s character or competence is directly at issue.
When character is the issue, all three proof methods may enter.

sections.title: Character in Civil Cases
body: In civil cases, character evidence is generally inadmissible to prove conforming conduct.
The fact that the alleged conduct could also be criminal does not automatically trigger the criminal-case character exceptions.
In a negligence case, the plaintiff generally cannot prove that a driver caused the collision merely by showing the driver has a reputation for carelessness.
Civil propensity evidence generally stays out.

sections.title: Civil Self-Defense and Aggression
body: In a civil assault or battery action involving self-defense:
A defendant may offer evidence of the plaintiff’s violent character to show reasonable fear if the defendant knew of that character
When the identity of the first aggressor is disputed, some courts allow evidence of the plaintiff’s violent character or the defendant’s peacefulness
Violence evidence may matter to reasonable fear or first aggression, not general badness.

sections.title: Criminal Defendant’s Character
body: A criminal defendant may introduce evidence of a pertinent good-character trait.
The prosecution may not begin with propensity evidence of the defendant’s bad character.
A defendant charged with embezzlement may offer reputation or opinion testimony for honesty.
A reputation for athletic ability would not be pertinent.
The defendant may open the character door with a trait related to the charge.

sections.title: Prosecution Rebuttal of Defendant Character
body: Once the defendant offers evidence of a pertinent good trait, the prosecution may rebut with evidence of the opposite trait.
The prosecution may also cross-examine the character witness about relevant specific acts with a good-faith basis.
Extrinsic evidence may not be used merely to prove those acts after the witness denies them.
Opening the character door lets the prosecution walk through the same doorway.

sections.title: Victim Character in Criminal Cases
body: A criminal defendant may offer reputation or opinion evidence of a pertinent bad trait of the alleged victim.
Evidence of the victim’s good character for the same trait
Evidence of the defendant’s bad character for that same trait
A defendant claiming self-defense offers testimony that the alleged victim had a reputation for violence.
The prosecution may respond with evidence that the victim was peaceful and that the defendant was violent.
Attacking the victim’s trait can expose the defendant’s matching trait.

sections.title: Peacefulness of a Homicide Victim
body: In a homicide case, when the defendant offers evidence that the victim was the first aggressor, the prosecution may offer evidence of the victim’s peaceful character.
The defendant need not first introduce formal character testimony.
First-aggressor evidence in homicide opens the victim-peacefulness response.

sections.title: Methods of Proving Character
body: When character is used circumstantially, proof is generally limited to:
When Character Is an Essential Element
Propensity exception usually means reputation or opinion; essential element permits specific acts too.

sections.title: Other Crimes, Wrongs, or Acts
body: A specific act may not be admitted merely to show propensity.
It may be admitted for a legitimate noncharacter purpose.
Use MIMIC, while remembering the list is not exclusive:
The prior act must prove something other than “the defendant is the type who does this.”

sections.title: Other-Acts Example
body: A manager is charged with setting fire to a warehouse to collect insurance proceeds.
Evidence that the manager recently increased the insurance coverage may prove motive and preparation.
The evidence is not offered merely to show bad character.

body: Is it relevant to a proper nonpropensity purpose?
Is there enough evidence for a jury to find the act occurred?
Should the court give a limiting instruction?
In a criminal case, did the prosecution provide proper notice?
Proper purpose, sufficient proof, Rule 403, notice, limiting instruction.

sections.title: Notice of Other-Acts Evidence
body: In a criminal case, the prosecution generally must give reasonable written notice before trial of:
The court may excuse pretrial notice for good cause.
The prosecutor must identify not only the act, but the nonpropensity reasoning.

sections.title: Cross-Examining a Character Witness
body: A character witness may be asked about relevant specific acts of the person whose character the witness described.
The examiner must have a good-faith basis.
The purpose is to test the witness’s knowledge or basis for the character opinion.
If the witness denies the act, extrinsic evidence is generally not admissible merely to prove it.
Ask the character witness about the act; do not launch a side trial to prove it.

sections.title: Habit
body: Habit is a regular, specific, nearly automatic response to a repeated situation.
Habit evidence is admissible to prove conduct in conformity with the habit.
It does not require corroboration or an eyewitness.
“Every evening, scans the identification twice before unlocking the vault” suggests habit
A laboratory technician follows the same five-step contamination protocol before every test.
That routine may be admitted to show the technician followed it on the day in question.
Character is general disposition; habit is a specific repeated reflex.

sections.title: Witness Competency
body: Every person is competent to testify if the person:
Takes an oath or affirmation to testify truthfully
Mental limitations, intoxication, age, or memory weaknesses usually affect credibility rather than competency.
In a civil case, state law governs witness competency when state law supplies the rule of decision.
Federal competency is broad; weaknesses usually go to weight.

sections.title: Children and Mental Capacity
body: No fixed age automatically makes a child incompetent.
The court may examine whether the witness:
Understands the duty to tell the truth
Competency depends on ability, not birthday.

sections.title: Personal Knowledge
body: A lay witness may testify only to matters the witness personally perceived.
Personal knowledge may be established through the witness’s own testimony.
The judge asks whether a reasonable jury could find personal knowledge.
A witness repeating a hearsay statement needs personal knowledge that the statement was made, not personal knowledge of the facts asserted.
A witness must know the event firsthand—or at least know firsthand that the statement occurred.

sections.title: Oath or Affirmation
body: Every witness must give an oath or affirmation designed to impress upon the witness the duty to testify truthfully.
An interpreter must also promise to translate truthfully.
The form may vary; the conscious duty to tell the truth may not.

sections.title: Judge as Witness
body: The presiding judge may not testify as a witness in the trial.
No objection is required to preserve the error.
The judge cannot occupy the bench and witness stand in the same trial.

sections.title: Juror as Witness During Trial
body: A juror may not testify as a witness before the other jurors in the same trial.
If a juror is called, the opposing party must have an opportunity to object outside the jury’s presence.
A sitting juror cannot become ordinary trial evidence before the panel.

sections.title: No-Impeachment Rule for Verdicts
body: After a verdict, a juror generally may not testify about:
A clerical mistake in entering the verdict
A constitutional exception also exists for a clear statement that racial animus was a significant motivating factor in a criminal conviction.
A juror may testify that someone threatened a juror’s family.
A juror generally may not testify that another juror misunderstood the instructions.
Outside influence may be exposed; internal reasoning usually remains sealed.

sections.title: Dead Man’s Statutes
body: The Federal Rules contain no Dead Man’s Statute.
A state statute may apply in a federal civil case when state law supplies the rule of decision.
Such statutes commonly restrict an interested person from testifying about transactions or communications with a deceased person when the estate is an opposing party.
No federal Dead Man’s rule, but Erie may bring a state one into a diversity case.

sections.title: Impeachment: Big Picture
body: Any party may impeach any witness, including the party who called the witness.
Impeachment asks why this witness should not be believed.

sections.title: Reputation and Opinion for Untruthfulness
body: A witness’s credibility may be attacked through reputation or opinion testimony that the witness has a character for untruthfulness.
Evidence of truthful character is admissible only after the witness’s character for truthfulness has been attacked.
An attack based only on bias or faulty perception does not automatically open the door to truthful-character evidence.
Truthful-character rehabilitation requires an attack on truthfulness itself.

sections.title: Specific Acts Showing Untruthfulness
body: On cross-examination, the court may allow questions about a specific act that is probative of:
The witness’s character for truthfulness, or
Another witness’s truthfulness when the witness gave character testimony about that person
The examiner must have a good-faith basis.
If the witness denies the act, extrinsic evidence generally may not be introduced solely to prove that act for truthfulness impeachment.
A witness may be asked whether the witness falsified expense reports.
If denied, the examiner generally cannot introduce the employer’s disciplinary file solely to prove the lie.
Ask about dishonest conduct on cross, but accept the answer unless another impeachment ground applies.

sections.title: Arrests
body: An arrest is not itself misconduct and generally may not be used to impeach truthfulness.
The underlying conduct may be asked about if it is probative of truthfulness and Rule 608 permits the inquiry.
Arrest proves accusation, not dishonest conduct.

sections.title: Convictions Involving Dishonesty or False Statement
body: Convictions Involving Dishonesty or False Statement
A conviction must be admitted for impeachment when proving the offense required establishing a dishonest act or false statement.
A crime is not automatically a dishonesty crime merely because deception occurred during its commission.
If deceit is an element, the conviction comes in subject to the time limit.

sections.title: Felony Convictions Not Involving Dishonesty
body: A felony conviction is admissible only when its probative value outweighs its prejudicial effect to that defendant.
The conviction is generally admissible subject to ordinary Rule 403, meaning the opponent must show prejudice substantially outweighs probative value.
Criminal defendant gets the protective balance; other witnesses get ordinary Rule 403.

sections.title: Convictions More Than Ten Years Old
body: Convictions More Than Ten Years Old
The ten-year period runs from the later of:
Release from confinement for that conviction
An older conviction is admissible only if:
Its probative value substantially outweighs prejudice
Old convictions face reverse-403 protection and notice.

sections.title: Pardons, Juvenile Adjudications, and Appeals
body: A conviction is generally inadmissible after a pardon based on innocence.
A rehabilitation-based pardon may also exclude it unless a later serious conviction exists.
They generally cannot impeach a criminal defendant.
They may sometimes impeach another witness in a criminal case when necessary to fairly determine guilt or innocence.
A conviction may be used while appeal is pending.
The pendency of the appeal may also be shown.
Pending appeal does not erase the conviction; juvenile and pardoned matters receive special protection.

sections.title: Proving a Conviction
body: A conviction may be proved by:
Unlike Rule 608 specific acts, extrinsic proof is allowed.
Convictions are the major extrinsic-evidence exception for truthfulness impeachment.

sections.title: Prior Inconsistent Statements for Impeachment
body: A witness may be impeached with a prior statement inconsistent with current testimony.
The examiner need not first show the statement to the witness, but must show or disclose it to opposing counsel upon request.
Extrinsic evidence generally may not be admitted until:
The witness has an opportunity to explain or deny it
The opposing party has an opportunity to examine the witness about it
Exceptions apply to opposing-party statements, hearsay declarants, or when justice requires.
Confront the witness before admitting extrinsic proof of the inconsistency.

sections.title: Substantive Use of Prior Inconsistent Statements
body: Substantive Use of Prior Inconsistent Statements
A prior inconsistent statement is admissible for its truth when:
The declarant is subject to cross-examination
The prior statement was made under penalty of perjury at a trial, hearing, proceeding, or deposition
An ordinary unsworn statement generally serves only to impeach unless another hearsay rule applies.
Under oath in a formal proceeding makes the inconsistency substantive evidence.

sections.title: Bias or Interest
body: Bias is always relevant to credibility.
Extrinsic evidence of bias is generally admissible.
Many courts require that the witness first receive an opportunity to address the alleged bias.
Bias is never collateral to credibility.

sections.title: Sensory Competence
body: A witness may be impeached by evidence showing inability to:
Evidence that a witness was 200 feet away in dense fog may undermine identification testimony.
Challenge the witness’s ability to perceive, remember, or report.

sections.title: Impeaching a Hearsay Declarant
body: When a hearsay statement is admitted, the declarant may be impeached as though the declarant had testified.
The declarant need not receive an opportunity to explain a prior inconsistent statement.
If the opponent later calls the declarant, the opponent may examine the declarant as on cross-examination concerning the statement.
An absent declarant’s credibility is not immune from attack.

sections.title: Rehabilitation
body: A witness may be rehabilitated through:
Reputation or opinion testimony for truthfulness after a truthfulness attack
Evidence correcting a sensory or contradiction attack
Rehabilitation must respond to the form of impeachment.
Repair the specific damage; do not generally bolster before an attack.

sections.title: Prior Consistent Statements
body: A prior consistent statement may be admitted for its truth when:
The declarant is subject to cross-examination
The statement rebuts fabrication or improper motive, or rehabilitates after another qualifying attack
To rebut a charge of recent fabrication or improper motive, the statement must have been made before that motive arose.
A statement made before the alleged motive can show the story did not begin with the motive.

sections.title: Religious Beliefs
body: Religious beliefs or opinions may not be used to attack or support credibility.
They may be admitted for another purpose, such as bias.
A witness’s religious affiliation cannot be offered to show honesty.
It may be relevant if the witness belongs to an organization that is financially supporting a party.
Religion cannot prove truthfulness, but a relationship may prove bias.

sections.title: Impeachment by Contradiction
body: Evidence contradicting a witness may impeach credibility.
Extrinsic evidence is generally barred when the contradiction concerns a collateral matter.
A matter is collateral when it has no relevance except to show that the witness was wrong.
Do not create a mini-trial over an irrelevant detail merely to show a witness made a mistake.

sections.title: Present Recollection Refreshed
body: Any item may be used to refresh a witness’s memory.
The item need not be admissible.
After reviewing it, the witness must testify from refreshed present memory rather than read the item aloud.
When a writing is used while testifying, the opponent may:
Request removal of unrelated material through in-camera review
The item awakens memory; the testimony comes from the witness.

sections.title: Past Recollection Recorded
body: A record may be read into evidence when:
The witness once knew the matter
The witness now cannot recall well enough to testify fully
The record was made or adopted while the matter was fresh
The record accurately reflected the witness’s knowledge
The proponent may have it read to the jury.
The record becomes an exhibit only if the adverse party offers it.
Recorded recollection speaks when memory cannot be refreshed.

sections.title: Lay Opinion
body: A lay witness may give an opinion when it is:
Rationally based on the witness’s perception
Helpful to understanding testimony or deciding a fact
Not based on scientific, technical, or specialized knowledge
Lay opinion summarizes ordinary firsthand perception.

sections.title: Expert Qualification
body: A witness may qualify as an expert through:
A formal degree is not always required.
Expertise comes from reliable specialized knowledge, not a particular job title.

blocks.text: A witness may qualify as an expert through:

sections.title: Expert Testimony Requirements
body: The proponent must show it is more likely than not that:
The expert’s specialized knowledge will help the factfinder
The testimony is based on sufficient facts or data
The testimony is produced by reliable principles and methods
The expert reliably applied those principles and methods to the case
Helpful, sufficient data, reliable method, reliable application.

sections.title: Reliability Factors
body: Depending on the field, the court may consider:
Whether the method can be tested
Whether it has been peer reviewed
Whether the expert used the same care outside litigation
Whether the opinion was developed only for the lawsuit
No single factor is mandatory in every case.
The reliability inquiry fits the field, not a rigid checklist.

sections.title: Basis of Expert Opinion
body: An expert may base an opinion on:
Information made known before or during trial
The underlying information need not itself be admissible if experts in the field reasonably rely on that type of information.
If the underlying information is inadmissible, the proponent may disclose it to the jury only when its probative value in evaluating the opinion substantially outweighs its prejudicial effect.
Experts may rely on more than jurors may hear.

sections.title: Expert Testimony and Ultimate Issues
body: An opinion is not objectionable merely because it embraces an ultimate issue.
An expert may not state whether the criminal defendant did or did not possess a mental state or condition constituting an element or defense.
The expert may not give the final conclusion about the defendant’s own required mental state.
An expert may testify that most people carrying a certain quantity of contraband know it is present.
The expert may not testify, “This defendant knowingly possessed it.”
Experts may approach the ultimate issue, but not decide the criminal defendant’s required mental state.

sections.title: Expert Disclosure of Underlying Facts
body: An expert may state an opinion without first disclosing all supporting facts.
On cross-examination, the opponent may require disclosure of those facts.
A hypothetical question is not required.
The opinion may come first; cross-examination can expose the foundation.

sections.title: Court-Appointed Experts and Interpreters
body: The court may appoint an expert.
An interpreter is treated under the expert rules and must promise a true translation.
A court expert remains subject to adversarial testing.

sections.title: Paying Witnesses
body: A witness may not be paid based on:
Pay for time and expertise, never for a desired answer or victory.

sections.title: Authentication
body: The proponent must introduce evidence sufficient to support a finding that an item is what the proponent claims it is.
Authentication does not conclusively establish genuineness.
The opponent may still challenge the item before the jury.
Authentication gets the item through the door; the jury may still reject it as false.

body: A physical item may be authenticated through:
An owner identifies a rare guitar by a repaired crack and custom engraving.
That testimony may authenticate the guitar.
Show the object’s identity through recognition, features, or custody.

sections.title: Chain of Custody
body: Chain of custody is important for fungible or easily altered evidence, such as:
The proponent need not eliminate every theoretical possibility of tampering.
Reasonable assurance of identity and unchanged condition is enough.
Fungible evidence needs a reliable journey from collection to courtroom.

sections.title: Photographs and Videos
body: A photograph or video may be authenticated by a witness who knows that it fairly and accurately depicts the scene or event.
The photographer or camera operator usually need not testify.
Automated recordings may be authenticated by showing the recording process was reliable.
A knowledgeable witness or a reliable process can authenticate the image.

body: For an image of something not visible to ordinary perception, the proponent generally shows:
When no eyewitness can verify accuracy, authenticate the machine and process.

sections.title: Documentary Authentication
body: Authenticate documents through origin, appearance, response, custody, or process.

sections.title: Ancient-Document Authentication
body: A document may be authenticated as ancient when it:
Is in a condition creating no suspicion
Was found where an authentic document of that type would likely be kept
Ancient-document authentication is broader than the ancient-document hearsay exception.
The hearsay exception generally applies only to qualifying statements in documents prepared before January 1, 1998.
Twenty years may authenticate; pre-1998 is needed for the ancient-document hearsay exception.

body: An expert or the jury may compare disputed handwriting with an authenticated sample.
A lay witness may identify handwriting based on familiarity not acquired for the litigation.
Lay familiarity must predate the lawsuit; experts and jurors may compare exemplars.

sections.title: Voice and Telephone Calls
body: A person may identify a voice after hearing it at any time, including for litigation purposes.
A call may be authenticated when:
The witness called an assigned number and the answerer identified themselves
The witness called a business and discussed ordinary business with the person answering
Voice familiarity may be acquired at any time; context can authenticate the call.

sections.title: Electronic Communications and Social Media
body: Electronic evidence may be authenticated through:
An account name or profile photograph alone may be insufficient if authorship is genuinely disputed.
A message contains a private nickname, refers to a meeting only two people knew about, and was sent from a device seized from the alleged sender.
Together, those facts may authenticate it.
Authenticate the person behind the account, not merely the account’s label.

body: No extrinsic authentication is generally required for:
Items declared authentic by federal statute
The proponent generally must provide reasonable written notice and make the record available for inspection.
Self-authentication removes the foundation witness, not other objections such as hearsay.

sections.title: Best Evidence Rule
body: To prove the contents of a writing, recording, or photograph, the proponent generally must produce:
The rule applies when the contents themselves matter.
The rule does not require a document merely because one exists.
A witness with personal knowledge may testify about an event without producing notes made about the event.
The rule governs proof of contents, not proof of every event that was recorded.

sections.title: When Contents Are at Issue
body: The best evidence rule commonly applies when:
The document has legal effect, such as a contract or will
A photograph or video is offered to prove what it depicts
A witness learned the fact only by reading the document
A party claims a lease contained a five-year renewal option.
Because the precise lease terms are at issue, the original or a qualifying substitute should be produced.
Exact contents trigger the original-document rule.

sections.title: Originals and Duplicates
body: For electronically stored information, any accurate readable output may qualify as an original.
A photograph’s negative and prints from it may qualify as originals.
A duplicate produced by an accurate process is generally admissible like the original unless:
Authenticity of the original is genuinely disputed
A handwritten copy is not ordinarily a duplicate.
Accurate mechanical copy usually equals original; handwritten copy does not.

sections.title: When the Original Is Unavailable
body: Other evidence of contents may be used when:
Originals were lost or destroyed without bad faith
The original cannot be obtained by judicial process
The opponent controlled it, received notice, and failed to produce it
The content concerns only a collateral matter
No hierarchy requires the next-best substitute.
Account honestly for the original, then prove contents by another reliable method.

sections.title: Public Records and the Best Evidence Rule
body: Public Records and the Best Evidence Rule
The contents of an official record may generally be proved by:
A copy compared with the original by a witness
Other evidence if a certified or compared copy cannot be obtained with reasonable diligence
Do not remove the government’s original file when a certified copy will do.

sections.title: Rule 1006 Summaries
body: A summary, chart, or calculation may be admitted to prove the contents of voluminous admissible records that cannot conveniently be examined in court.
The proponent must make the originals or duplicates available to the opponent at a reasonable time and place.
The underlying records need not themselves be admitted, but they must be admissible.
Rule 107 illustrative aid is not evidence
A Rule 1006 summary replaces mountains of admissible records; an illustrative aid merely explains.

sections.title: Opposing Party’s Admission of Contents
body: The contents of a writing, recording, or photograph may be proved through the opposing party’s:
The original need not be accounted for.
An informal oral statement made outside testimony or deposition does not receive the same Rule 1007 treatment.
A party’s formal admission can prove contents without the original.

sections.title: Judge and Jury Under the Best Evidence Rule
body: Judge and Jury Under the Best Evidence Rule
The judge generally decides whether the conditions for secondary evidence are satisfied.
Whether the asserted original ever existed
Whether an item is the original
Whether other evidence correctly reflects the contents
The judge decides admissibility conditions; the jury resolves authenticity-of-content disputes.

sections.title: Parol Evidence Rule
body: The parol evidence rule is a substantive contract rule that may exclude prior or contemporaneous evidence offered to alter an integrated writing.
Consistent additional terms may be admitted, but contradictory terms may not.
Evidence may be admitted to show:
The rule limits earlier agreement evidence, not later modification evidence.

sections.title: Demonstrative and Experimental Evidence
body: A court may permit demonstrations, experiments, models, or exhibitions when they fairly assist the factfinder.
The court may exclude them when:
A courtroom experiment must fairly recreate what it claims to demonstrate.

sections.title: Privilege: General Rule
body: A privilege protects certain confidential communications from compelled disclosure.
Federal common law generally governs privilege in federal-question cases.
State privilege law generally governs a civil claim or defense for which state law supplies the rule of decision.
A privilege usually requires an intent that the communication remain confidential.
Necessary third parties, such as interpreters or legal assistants, generally do not destroy confidentiality.
Privilege protects confidential relationships, not underlying facts.

sections.title: Waiver of Privilege
body: A privilege may be waived by:
Voluntary disclosure of a substantial portion
An unauthorized disclosure generally does not waive the holder’s privilege.
The holder controls the privilege and may lose it through voluntary disclosure.

sections.title: Spousal Immunity
body: In federal criminal proceedings, the witness-spouse may refuse to testify adversely against the defendant-spouse.
Valid marriage must exist when testimony is sought
Covers events before and during marriage
A spouse may refuse to testify at the other spouse’s criminal trial about conduct observed before their wedding, as long as they remain married at trial.
Spousal immunity protects the marriage at the time of testimony.

sections.title: Confidential Marital Communications
body: A confidential communication made between spouses during a valid marriage is privileged.
Applies in civil and criminal cases
Covers only communications, not every observed act
Communication must be made during marriage
Generally either spouse may assert it
The communication must occur during marriage, but the protection may last after marriage.

sections.title: Marital Privilege Exceptions
body: The marital privileges generally do not apply in proceedings involving:
Crime by one spouse against the other
Crime against a child of either spouse
Joint criminal activity in some jurisdictions
Marriage privilege does not ordinarily shield abuse within the family relationship.

sections.title: Attorney-Client Privilege
body: The privilege protects a communication that is:
Between attorney and client or their necessary representatives
Made to obtain or provide legal assistance
It generally survives termination of the relationship and the client’s death.
Confidential legal communication—not every interaction with a lawyer—is privileged.

sections.title: What Attorney-Client Privilege Does Not Protect
body: What Attorney-Client Privilege Does Not Protect
The privilege generally does not protect:
Communications for business rather than legal advice
Communications intended to be shared with outsiders
A lawyer acting only as a witness, business partner, or negotiator
A client cannot be forced to disclose what legal advice the lawyer gave.
The client may still be required to disclose where the client placed a document if that is an independently known fact.
Telling a fact to a lawyer does not turn the fact itself into a secret.

sections.title: Corporate Attorney-Client Privilege
body: The privilege may cover employee communications when:
The employee speaks at corporate direction
The subject concerns the employee’s corporate duties
The purpose is to obtain legal advice for the corporation
The privilege is not limited to top executives.
Corporate privilege follows legal purpose and job-related information, not only the control group.

sections.title: Attorney-Client Exceptions
body: The privilege does not protect communications:
Made to further a crime or fraud
Relevant to a dispute between lawyer and client
Relevant to a dispute among claimants through the same deceased client
Between former joint clients in later litigation between them
The client need not successfully complete the crime or fraud.
The communication must seek help advancing or concealing wrongful conduct, not merely discuss past wrongdoing.
Lawyers may advise about past misconduct; privilege does not protect plans for future crime or fraud.

sections.title: Inadvertent Disclosure Under Rule 502
body: An inadvertent disclosure in a federal proceeding does not waive attorney-client privilege or work-product protection when the holder:
Took reasonable steps to prevent disclosure
Promptly took reasonable steps to correct it
An intentional disclosure ordinarily waives protection for the disclosed material.
Subject-matter waiver extends to undisclosed material only when fairness requires it because selective disclosure would be misleading.
Reasonable prevention plus prompt correction can preserve privilege after an accident.

sections.title: Federal Nonwaiver Orders
body: A federal court may order that disclosure in the pending litigation does not waive privilege or work-product protection in any federal or state proceeding.
A private agreement ordinarily binds only the parties unless incorporated into a court order.
A Rule 502(d) order can provide broader protection than a private clawback agreement.

sections.title: Work-Product Doctrine
body: Work product includes documents and tangible things prepared in anticipation of litigation by or for a party or representative.
Inability without undue hardship to obtain the substantial equivalent
An attorney’s mental impressions, conclusions, opinions, and strategies receive nearly absolute protection.
Attorney-client protects communications; work product protects litigation preparation.

sections.title: Physician-Patient Privilege
body: There is no general federal common-law physician-patient privilege.
Many states create one by statute.
A state privilege may apply in a federal civil case governed by state substantive law.
Medical hearsay exception is federal; medical privilege usually depends on state law.

sections.title: Psychotherapist-Patient Privilege
body: Federal law recognizes a privilege for confidential communications with:
The communication must occur during diagnosis or treatment.
Common exceptions include court-ordered examinations and proceedings placing the mental condition directly at issue.
Federal law recognizes psychotherapy confidentiality even though it lacks a general physician privilege.

sections.title: Fifth Amendment Privilege
body: A person may refuse compelled testimonial communication that could reasonably incriminate that person.
The privilege protects compelled incriminating testimony, not physical identity evidence.

sections.title: Adverse Inferences and Immunity
body: The prosecution may not comment on the defendant’s silence or ask the jury to draw an adverse inference.
An adverse inference may generally be drawn from a valid assertion of the privilege.
The government may compel testimony by granting use and derivative-use immunity.
The government may still prosecute using evidence derived from an independent source.
No criminal penalty for silence; civil juries may draw an inference.

sections.title: Other Privileges
body: There is no general federal constitutional privilege allowing journalists to refuse every demand to identify sources.
Many additional privileges depend on jurisdiction rather than a uniform federal rule.

sections.title: Subsequent Remedial Measures
body: A measure taken after an injury that would have made the injury less likely is inadmissible to prove:
The evidence may be used, when disputed, to prove:
The measure must occur after the injury
A measure by an unrelated third party is generally not excluded by Rule 407
Later safety improvements do not prove earlier fault.

sections.title: Compromise Offers and Negotiations
body: Evidence of settlement offers, compromise conduct, and statements made during negotiations is inadmissible to prove:
There must be an actual dispute about validity or amount.
The claim must be disputed before Rule 408 protection begins.

blocks.text: Evidence of settlement offers, compromise conduct, and statements made during negotiations is inadmissible to prove:

sections.title: Settlement Discussions Do Not Immunize Existing Evidence
body: Settlement Discussions Do Not Immunize Existing Evidence
A party cannot protect otherwise discoverable or admissible evidence merely by mentioning it during settlement talks.
An internal safety report does not become inadmissible simply because counsel attached it to a settlement email.
Negotiations are protected; preexisting evidence is not transformed by entering the room.

sections.title: Offers to Pay Medical Expenses
body: An offer or payment of medical expenses is inadmissible to prove liability.
Unlike settlement negotiations, accompanying factual admissions are not automatically excluded.
“I will pay your hospital bill” is excluded to prove fault.
“I was reading a text and never saw the red light; I will pay your hospital bill” contains an accompanying admission that may be admissible.
The payment offer is excluded; the attached admission may remain.

sections.title: Pleas and Plea Discussions
body: Statements during plea negotiations with a prosecutor
Fairness requires completing a partially introduced plea statement
Later perjury prosecution based on a false statement made under oath, on the record, with counsel present
Failed plea discussions generally cannot become prosecution evidence.

sections.title: Liability Insurance
body: Evidence that a person has or lacks liability insurance is inadmissible to prove negligence or wrongful conduct.
An insurance adjuster’s employment may be shown to establish bias, even though the policy cannot be used to prove the insured acted negligently.
Insurance cannot prove fault, but it may prove relationship or bias.

sections.title: Rape Shield Rule
body: In a proceeding involving alleged sexual misconduct, evidence of an alleged victim’s other sexual behavior or sexual predisposition is generally inadmissible.
The rule applies to substantive evidence and impeachment.
The victim’s unrelated sexual history is ordinarily off limits.

blocks.text: In a proceeding involving alleged sexual misconduct, evidence of an alleged victim’s other sexual behavior or sexual predisposition is generally inadmissible.

sections.title: Rape Shield Exceptions in Criminal Cases
body: Rape Shield Exceptions in Criminal Cases
Specific sexual-behavior evidence may be admitted to prove:
Someone other than the defendant caused semen, injury, or physical evidence
Sexual behavior with the defendant, when offered to prove consent or offered by the prosecution
Exclusion would violate the defendant’s constitutional rights
Reputation and opinion evidence are generally not admissible under these criminal exceptions.
Alternative source, consent with accused, or constitutional necessity.

sections.title: Rape Shield in Civil Cases
body: Evidence of a victim’s sexual behavior or predisposition is admissible only when its probative value substantially outweighs:
Reputation evidence is admissible only if the victim placed reputation in controversy.
Civil sexual-history evidence faces a reverse-403 test.

sections.title: Rape Shield Procedure
body: File a motion describing the evidence and purpose
File at least 14 days before trial unless the court changes the time
The hearing record is generally sealed.
Sexual-history evidence requires advance motion and private judicial screening.

blocks.text: File a motion describing the evidence and purpose

sections.title: Defendant’s Other Sexual Assaults or Child Molestation
body: Defendant’s Other Sexual Assaults or Child Molestation

In a case alleging sexual assault or child molestation, evidence that the defendant committed another sexual assault or act of child molestation may be admitted for any relevant purpose, including propensity.
This is a major exception to ordinary character rules.
The evidence is still subject to Rule 403.
The prosecution or civil plaintiff generally must disclose the evidence at least 15 days before trial unless good cause permits later disclosure.
Sex-offense cases permit propensity evidence that Rule 404 ordinarily forbids.

sections.title: Hearsay Definition
body: Made by a declarant outside the current trial or hearing
Offered to prove the truth of what the statement asserts
Hearsay is generally inadmissible unless a rule, statute, or Supreme Court rule permits it.
Out of court + assertion + offered for truth = hearsay.

sections.title: Declarant Must Be a Person
body: Machine output and animal behavior are not hearsay unless they communicate a human assertion.
Examples of nonhearsay machine or animal output include:
Authentication and reliability objections may still apply.
Machines may malfunction, but they do not make hearsay statements on their own.

sections.title: What Counts as a Statement
body: Nonverbal conduct intended as an assertion
Nonassertive conduct is not a statement.
A person points toward a suspect in response to “Who did it?” That is assertive conduct.
A person opens an umbrella outside is not ordinarily asserting that it is raining.
Conduct becomes hearsay only when intended to communicate an assertion.

sections.title: Verbal Acts and Legally Operative Language
body: Verbal Acts and Legally Operative Language
Words with independent legal significance are not offered for their truth.
In a breach case, “I accept your offer” is admitted to show contract formation, not to prove the truth of a factual assertion.
When speaking the words creates legal rights, the words are not hearsay.

sections.title: Effect on the Listener
body: A statement offered to show its effect on the person who heard it is not hearsay.
A security guard is told, “The rear door alarm is broken.”
The statement may be offered to prove the guard had notice, whether or not the alarm was actually broken.
Sometimes the important fact is that the words were heard, not that they were true.

sections.title: Circumstantial Evidence of State of Mind
body: Circumstantial Evidence of State of Mind
A statement may be admitted not for its truth, but as circumstantial evidence of the declarant’s mental condition.
A person repeatedly says, “Invisible satellites are controlling my hands.”
The statements may be offered to show delusion, not to prove satellites existed.
A false statement may still reveal the speaker’s mind.

sections.title: Statements Used for Identification or Connection
body: Statements Used for Identification or Connection
A statement or document may be admitted as circumstantial evidence connecting a person to a place, event, or object without relying on the truth asserted.
A restaurant reservation card bearing a defendant’s nickname is found in a getaway vehicle.
It may connect the defendant to the vehicle even if no assertion on the card is offered for truth.
A statement can function as a clue rather than a factual claim.

sections.title: Statements for Impeachment
body: A statement offered solely to show inconsistency and attack credibility is not offered for truth.
It may still require a limiting instruction.
If the statement independently qualifies as substantive evidence, the jury may use it for both purposes.
Impeachment use is nonhearsay; substantive use needs an additional rule.

sections.title: Multiple Hearsay
body: Hearsay within hearsay is admissible only when every layer is independently admissible.
A store incident report quotes a customer saying, “The employee pushed me.”
The report may qualify as a business record, but the customer’s statement needs a separate exception or nonhearsay purpose.
Peel every hearsay layer and justify each one.

sections.title: Declarant-Witness Prior Statements
body: Three categories are defined as nonhearsay when the declarant:
Testifies at the current trial or hearing
Is subject to cross-examination about the statement
The declarant must appear and face cross-examination.

sections.title: Prior Inconsistent Statement as Nonhearsay
body: A prior inconsistent statement is substantive nonhearsay when made under penalty of perjury at:
A witness testified under oath at a suppression hearing that the light was green.
At trial, the witness says it was red.
The earlier statement may be used for both impeachment and truth.
Formal sworn inconsistency is substantive evidence.

blocks.text: A prior inconsistent statement is substantive nonhearsay when made under penalty of perjury at:
A witness testified under oath at a suppression hearing that the light was green.
At trial, the witness says it was red.
The earlier statement may be used for both impeachment and truth.
Formal sworn inconsistency is substantive evidence.

sections.title: Prior Consistent Statement as Nonhearsay
body: A qualifying prior consistent statement may be admitted for its truth to:
Rebut recent fabrication or improper motive
Rehabilitate after another attack on credibility
When used to rebut motive or fabrication, it must predate the alleged motive.

blocks.text: A qualifying prior consistent statement may be admitted for its truth to:

sections.title: Prior Identification
body: A witness’s prior identification after perceiving a person is nonhearsay when:
The witness is subject to cross-examination
The witness need not remember the identification at trial.
A witness cannot remember the lineup at trial but is present and available for cross-examination.
An officer may testify that the witness identified the defendant at the lineup.
Prior identification is often more reliable than an in-court identification made much later.

sections.title: Opposing Party’s Statement
body: A party’s statement is nonhearsay when offered against that party.
Need not be against interest when made
Need not be based on personal knowledge
May have been made in an individual or representative capacity
A party cannot offer their own out-of-court statement under this rule.
Your opponent may use your words against you; you cannot self-admit them.

sections.title: Judicial Admissions
body: A formal admission in the current case, such as a stipulation or unamended pleading admission, may be conclusive.
An admission from another proceeding is generally evidentiary rather than conclusive and may be explained.
Formal current-case admissions bind; ordinary past admissions may be rebutted.

sections.title: Adoptive Admissions
body: A party adopts another person’s statement by words, conduct, or sometimes silence.
The party heard and understood the accusation
The circumstances naturally called for denial
A reasonable innocent person would deny it
Post-Miranda silence generally cannot be used as an adoptive admission.
Silence adopts only when ordinary circumstances demand an answer.

sections.title: Authorized Statements
body: A statement made by a person authorized by a party to speak on a subject is admissible against the party.
A company authorizes its communications director to announce a product recall.
The director’s recall statement may be offered against the company.
Authority to speak can make the speaker’s words the party’s words.

sections.title: Employee and Agent Statements
body: A statement is admissible against a principal when it was:
Made by an employee or agent
About a matter within the scope of that relationship
The employee need not have authority to make official statements.
During employment, a delivery supervisor tells a customer that the company’s brakes have been failing for weeks.
The statement concerns the supervisor’s work and may be offered against the company.
During employment + within job scope = potential party statement.

sections.title: Co-Conspirator Statements
body: A statement is admissible against a conspirator when made:
The court must find the conspiracy and participation by a preponderance of the evidence.
The statement may be considered but cannot alone establish the necessary relationship.
During and advancing the conspiracy—not merely talking about it.

sections.title: Unavailability
body: A declarant is unavailable when the declarant:
Cannot testify because of death or serious condition
Is absent despite reasonable efforts to secure attendance
The proponent cannot create the unavailability to prevent testimony.
Unavailability must be genuine, and absence often requires reasonable efforts.

blocks.text: A declarant is unavailable when the declarant:

sections.title: Former Testimony
body: The testimony was given at a trial, hearing, or lawful deposition
The party against whom it is offered had an opportunity and similar motive to develop the testimony
In a civil case, the opportunity may have belonged to a predecessor in interest.
A witness testified at an earlier civil trial about the same machine failure and was cross-examined by a predecessor with the same financial interest.
If the witness later dies, the testimony may qualify.
Opportunity plus similar motive to examine makes former testimony reliable.

sections.title: Grand Jury Testimony
body: Grand jury testimony generally does not qualify as former testimony against a criminal defendant because the defendant had no opportunity to cross-examine.
It may qualify as a prior inconsistent statement when the declarant testifies at trial and is subject to cross-examination.
Grand jury testimony lacks the defendant’s earlier cross-examination.

sections.title: Dying Declaration
body: The declarant believed death was imminent
The statement concerns the cause or circumstances of the expected death
The declarant need not actually die.
Belief in imminent death + statement about its cause; homicide or civil only.

sections.title: Statement Against Interest
body: An unavailable declarant’s statement is admissible when, at the time it was made, it was so contrary to the declarant’s:
that a reasonable person would not have made it unless believing it true.
A statement exposing the declarant to criminal liability requires corroborating circumstances clearly indicating trustworthiness.
The statement must hurt the declarant when made—not merely help the party offering it now.

sections.title: Personal or Family History
body: An unavailable declarant’s statement may be admitted about:
The statement may concern the declarant or a person closely connected to the declarant’s family.
Family-history statements may survive when the knowledgeable family declarant cannot testify.

sections.title: Forfeiture by Wrongdoing
body: A party forfeits hearsay objections to a declarant’s statement when the party:
Wrongfully caused or acquiesced in causing unavailability
Acted with the intent to prevent the declarant from testifying
The wrongdoing need not itself be criminal.
A party who intentionally silences the witness cannot profit from the silence.

sections.title: Present Sense Impression
body: A statement describing or explaining an event or condition is admissible when made:
While watching traffic from a balcony, a person says, “The blue sedan is crossing the center line.”
The statement may be a present sense impression.
Describe it as it happens or immediately after.

body: A startling event or condition occurred
The declarant remained under the stress of excitement
The declarant may be a participant or bystander.
Time matters, but stress matters more.
Startling event + statement related to it + continuing stress.

sections.title: Then-Existing State of Mind
body: A statement of the declarant’s then-existing:
may be admitted to prove that condition or later conduct consistent with intent.
A statement of memory or belief generally cannot prove the remembered past fact, except in certain will cases.
“I plan to meet Rowan at the station tonight” may be used to show the declarant later went to the station.
“I met Rowan at the station yesterday” is ordinarily inadmissible to prove yesterday’s meeting under this exception.
Present condition and future intent, not backward-looking memory.

Describes medical history, symptoms, pain, or cause
The information is reasonably pertinent to diagnosis or treatment
The statement may be made to:
Statements assigning fault are generally not pertinent, although identity may matter in treatment of child abuse or similar circumstances.
Medical purpose creates reliability; cause enters when treatment reasonably needs it.

sections.title: Recorded Recollection Exception
body: A qualifying recorded recollection may be read into evidence when the witness cannot recall sufficiently, even after an attempt to refresh.
The proponent does not normally place the record itself in the jury’s hands.
Read by the proponent; exhibit only at the opponent’s request.

A custodian, qualified witness, or certification establishes the foundation
The opponent does not show untrustworthiness
Timely, knowledgeable, regular activity, regular practice, proper foundation, trustworthy.

sections.title: Outsider Statements Inside Business Records
body: A business record does not automatically admit a statement supplied by an outsider who had no business duty to report accurately.
The outsider statement needs its own hearsay exception or nonhearsay use.
A hotel log routinely records guest complaints.
The log itself may be a business record, but a guest’s accusation written in the log requires a separate hearsay basis.
The record keeper’s duty does not cure an outsider’s hearsay.

body: A document prepared primarily for litigation may lack the routine reliability required for the business-record exception.
The opponent may show untrustworthiness based on:
Routine operations create trust; lawsuit preparation creates suspicion.

body: The absence of an entry may prove that an event did not occur when:
Records of that type are regularly kept
The matter would ordinarily be recorded
The opponent does not show untrustworthiness
Silence in a complete routine record may speak.

body: Matters observed under a legal duty to report
Factual findings from a legally authorized investigation
The prosecution generally may not use law-enforcement observations under the public-record exception in a criminal case.
Against the government in criminal cases
Public duties create reliability, but prosecutors cannot use police observations through the public-record shortcut.

sections.title: Police Reports
body: A police report may be admissible in a civil case or when offered by a criminal defendant, subject to other rules.
A civilian statement within the report requires a separate hearsay basis.
The prosecution generally cannot use law-enforcement observations against a criminal defendant under the public-record exception.
Separate the officer’s record from the civilian’s quoted statement.

body: Testimony or certification that a diligent search found no public record may prove that:
A recorded event did not occur
In a criminal case, a prosecutor using a certification generally must give written notice at least 14 days before trial.
The defendant generally has seven days to object in writing.
A diligent search can prove official silence, with special criminal notice.

body: A record of birth, death, or marriage is admissible when reported to a public office under a legal duty.

sections.title: Learned Treatises
body: A statement in a reliable learned treatise may be admitted when:
An expert relies on it during direct examination, or it is called to the expert’s attention on cross
The publication is established as reliable authority
The statement may be read into evidence.
The publication itself is not received as an exhibit.
Read the authority to the jury; do not send the book into deliberations.

sections.title: Prior Judgment of Conviction
body: A final judgment of conviction is admissible to prove a fact essential to the judgment when:
The conviction followed trial or guilty plea
The offense was punishable by death or more than one year of imprisonment
It was not based on a no-contest plea
In a criminal case, the prosecution may use this exception only for a conviction against the current defendant when offered for a nonimpeachment purpose.
Serious final conviction may prove essential facts, but no-contest pleas are excluded.

sections.title: Residual Exception
body: A hearsay statement not covered elsewhere may be admitted when:
It has sufficient guarantees of trustworthiness under the totality of circumstances
It is more probative on the point than evidence reasonably obtainable elsewhere
The proponent gives reasonable notice of substance and declarant
The exception is not a broad license to ignore the specific hearsay rules.
Residual hearsay requires exceptional trustworthiness, necessity, and notice.

sections.title: Confrontation Clause
body: In a criminal prosecution, the Sixth Amendment generally bars testimonial hearsay offered against the defendant unless:
The defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine the declarant
The Clause applies only to testimonial statements.
It is separate from the hearsay rules.
A statement may satisfy a hearsay exception and still violate confrontation.
Hearsay exception asks reliability rules; confrontation asks whether the accused had the right to cross-examine.

sections.title: When the Declarant Testifies
body: The Confrontation Clause is generally satisfied when the declarant appears at trial and is subject to cross-examination about the statement.
The declarant’s lack of memory does not necessarily eliminate the opportunity for cross-examination.
Confrontation requires an opportunity to question, not guaranteed useful answers.

sections.title: Testimonial Statements
body: Statements are likely testimonial when created primarily to establish past facts for criminal prosecution, such as:
Formal police interrogation about past crime
Statements are less likely testimonial when made:
By very young children to teachers
For ordinary business rather than prosecution
Testimonial statements are formal substitutes for courtroom testimony.

sections.title: Police Questioning and Ongoing Emergencies
body: Nontestimonial when the primary purpose is resolving an ongoing emergency
Testimonial when the primary purpose is establishing past events for later prosecution
The analysis is objective and considers all surrounding circumstances.
During an active break-in, a caller tells dispatch that an armed person is still inside.
At the station hours later, the caller gives a detailed account of the completed crime.
Emergency assistance is not testimony; building a past-crime record usually is.

sections.title: Statements to Private Persons
body: Statements to people who are not law-enforcement officers are less likely to be testimonial.
A very young child’s statements to a teacher about abuse are rarely testimonial.
Private conversation is less likely to function as a substitute for trial testimony.

sections.title: Laboratory Reports and Substitute Experts
body: A formal forensic report prepared for prosecution may be testimonial.
The prosecution generally cannot avoid confrontation by having a substitute expert merely relay the absent analyst’s statements.
When an expert communicates an absent analyst’s assertions and those assertions support the opinion only if true, the assertions are being introduced for their truth.
A testifying expert may give an independent opinion based on personally observed or independently admissible facts, but may not serve as a conduit for testimonial statements of an absent analyst.
A substitute expert cannot become a speaking pipe for an absent forensic witness.

body: A defendant forfeits confrontation rights only when the defendant wrongfully caused the witness’s unavailability with the purpose of preventing testimony.
Merely causing the witness’s absence is not enough without that intent.
The defendant must intend to silence the witness, not merely cause the absence.

body: The Supreme Court has suggested that the historical dying-declaration exception may permit testimonial dying declarations despite the ordinary confrontation rule.
This remains a narrow historical exception.
Dying declarations may survive confrontation because the exception predates the Constitution.

sections.title: Face-to-Face Confrontation
body: The Confrontation Clause generally prefers face-to-face testimony.
A child witness may testify through special procedures when:
An important public interest is served
The court makes a case-specific finding of necessity
Reliability and adversarial testing remain protected
A general presumption of trauma is insufficient.
Remote confrontation requires necessity tailored to the particular witness.

body: Due Process Right to Present a Defense
Even a valid evidence rule may violate due process when applied arbitrarily or disproportionately to prevent a criminal defendant from presenting a meaningful defense.
Evidence rules cannot be applied so rigidly that they erase the defendant’s fair opportunity to defend.

sections.title: Key Balancing Tests
body: | Rule | Test | |---|---| | Ordinary Rule 403 | Exclude when danger substantially outweighs probative value | | Old conviction | Admit only when probative value substantially outweighs prejudice | | Civil rape-shield evidence | Admit only when probative value substantially outweighs harm and prejudice | | Inadmissible expert basis disclosed to jury | Disclose only when probative value substantially outweighs prejudice | | Criminal defendant’s ordinary felony conviction | Admit when probative value outweighs prejudice to defendant |
Watch which side of the scale receives the word “substantially.”

sections.title: Important Evidence Deadlines
body: 14 days before trial — Motion to offer sexual-behavior or predisposition evidence under Rule 412, unless adjusted
15 days before trial — Disclosure of other sexual-assault or child-molestation evidence, unless good cause
14 days before trial — Prosecutor’s notice of certification showing absence of a public record
7 days after receiving notice — Defendant’s written objection to that certification
Reasonable pretrial notice — Rule 404(b) other-acts evidence
Reasonable written notice — Self-authenticated business records
Reasonable written notice — Conviction older than ten years
Evidence notice rules often test 14 days, 15 days, or reasonable notice.

sections.title: Final MBE Quick Rules
body: Judge decides preliminary admissibility by preponderance.
Preserve admission error with timely specific objection.
Preserve exclusion error with offer of proof.
A limiting instruction is required upon timely request.
Completeness can overcome hearsay when fairness requires.
Civil judicial notice is conclusive; criminal notice is optional for jury.
Relevant means any tendency concerning a consequential fact.
Civil propensity character evidence is generally barred.
Criminal defendant may offer a pertinent good trait.
Other acts require a nonpropensity purpose.
Habit is specific and nearly automatic.
Rule 608 dishonest acts permit cross but no extrinsic proof.
Prior sworn formal inconsistency may be substantive.
Recorded recollection is read, not offered as the proponent’s exhibit.
Expert must use sufficient data, reliable methods, and reliable application.
Expert may not decide the criminal defendant’s required mental state.
Inadmissible basis reaches jury only under reverse Rule 403.
Authentication requires evidence sufficient to support a finding.
Chain of custody matters for fungible evidence.
Best evidence applies only when proving contents.
Rule 1006 summary is evidence; Rule 107 aid is not.
Federal common law usually governs federal privilege.
State privilege law may govern state-law claims.
Spousal immunity is criminal and ends with marriage.
Attorney-client protects legal communications, not facts.
Subsequent repairs do not prove fault.
Statements offered for notice, effect, or legal significance are not hearsay.
Party statements need not be against interest.
Employee statement must concern job scope and occur during relationship.
Co-conspirator statement must be during and in furtherance.
Every layer of multiple hearsay needs a rule.
Former testimony needs similar motive and opportunity.
Dying declaration is limited to homicide and civil cases.
Business records require routine creation and trustworthy sources.
Public-record limits protect criminal defendants from prosecution use of police observations.
Learned treatise is read but not admitted as an exhibit.
Confrontation applies only in criminal cases against the accused.
Testimonial hearsay generally requires unavailability and prior cross-examination.
Substitute experts cannot relay absent analysts’ testimonial assertions for truth.
Forfeiture requires intent to prevent testimony.

sections.title: Final One-Sentence Summary
body: Evidence law is a purpose-driven system: identify why the item is offered, then apply relevance, exclusion, hearsay, foundation, privilege, and constitutional rules in that order.
